guardrail_evaluations.explanation: The effective discount of 33.3% exceeds the mid-market soft ceiling of 20%. This is a yellow flag that should prompt the AE to justify the concession size in writing before the deal advances.

At 33.3%, the discount exceeds the 30% mid-market Finance approval threshold. This deal cannot proceed to signature without explicit Finance sign-off — the AE must escalate immediately given the 10-day close window.

The ramp schedule spans 12 months (months 1–12), far exceeding the 3-month mid-market threshold. Finance approval is required; this structure also raises ASC 606 revenue recognition complexity that accounting will need to review.

Estimated gross margin at the proposed price is approximately 10%, well below the 40% warn threshold. The deal is deeply margin-compressed even before accounting for the $40K waived implementation services.

At ~10% gross margin, this deal violates the absolute 25% margin floor and must be restructured before it can be re-quoted. This is a hard stop — no approval path exists at current economics; concessions must be reshaped.

alternative_structures.label: 36-Month Commit with Reduced Discount + Paid Implementation
alternative_structures.description: Extend term to 36 months, reduce headline discount to 20%, eliminate the waived implementation services (bill $40K as a separate professional services line), shorten the free period to 3 months, and remove the 90-day out clause. Expansion pricing lock through end of 2027 can be retained as a goodwill concession.
alternative_structures.rationale: Dropping the discount to 20% and adding back the $40K implementation line gets margin above the absolute floor. The 36-month term offsets the reduced headline discount by improving LTV and reducing churn risk — Tessera's consolidation intent and profitable quarters support long-term commitment. Removing the 90-day out clause is the structural win that makes the commitment credible.

alternative_structures.label: Year-1 Ramp with Built-In Escalator + Usage Floor
alternative_structures.description: Accept a 6-month ramp but charge a nominal ramp fee of $10K/month during months 1–6 (instead of $0), stepping to full $20K/month in months 7–12. Year 2 escalates 10% ($264K ACV). Remove the 90-day out clause. Implementation services billed at cost ($25K, split-funded). Headline discount reduced to 25%.
alternative_structures.rationale: The nominal ramp fee preserves revenue recognition simplicity, partially recovers the free-period giveaway, and signals to Tessera that the ramp is a scheduling accommodation rather than an extended trial. The Year 2 escalator provides ACV upside and sets a precedent for future expansion pricing. Margin remains below the absolute floor — this structure requires a waiver but is meaningfully better than the current proposal and more defensible with Finance.

alternative_structures.label: Co-Marketing Credit in Lieu of Additional Discount + Remove Out Clause
alternative_structures.description: Hold the headline discount at 25% (proposed price $270K), remove the 90-day termination-for-convenience right entirely, waive implementation services but offset with a formal co-marketing and case study commitment (Tessera participates in a joint press release, a published case study, and one analyst reference call within 12 months of go-live). Ramp shortened to 3 months (months 1–3 at $0, months 4–12 at full rate) to comply with mid-market ramp guardrail and simplify ASC 606.
alternative_structures.rationale: The co-marketing obligation converts a pure price concession into a pipeline asset — a healthtech reference customer at Tessera's ARR tier has material pipeline value. Removing the 90-day out clause is the single most important structural improvement; it reduces churn optionality the customer should not have at this discount depth. Shortening the ramp to 3 months clears the mid-market ramp guardrail and simplifies revenue recognition, removing one of the two block_without_approval flags.

pricing.reasoning_summary: This deal fails every guardrail simultaneously: the 33.3% discount violates both the mid-market warn (20%) and Finance sign-off (30%) thresholds; the 12-month ramp far exceeds the 3-month mid-market ceiling; and estimated gross margin of ~10% breaches the absolute 25% floor — making the deal non-executable as structured. Margin is computed assuming list price carries a 40% gross margin (COGS = 60% of list = $216K), which is a working assumption the CFO should validate; if COGS are lower, the margin picture improves proportionally. The $40K waived implementation services are an additional unmodeled concession that further erodes true deal economics beyond what is reflected in the margin estimate. Comparable precedent deals (Helix, Cipher, Tideline) all closed at 11–16% discount in adjacent segments, suggesting the 33.3% ask is a significant outlier that should be reanchored before Finance review.

performance_obligations.name: Platform subscription (paid term)
performance_obligations.description: 24-month SaaS subscription to the platform; paid term begins month 7 after a 6-month free period.
performance_obligations.rationale: Subscription access is a series of distinct services satisfied over time; customer simultaneously receives and consumes the benefit (ASC 606-10-25-27).

performance_obligations.name: 6-month free access period
performance_obligations.description: Free platform access for months 1–6 prior to the paid term commencing.
performance_obligations.rationale: Although billed at zero, the free period is a promised service and material right; under ASC 606 the total contract consideration ($480K TCV) should be allocated ratably across the entire 24-month service period (or 30 months including free), not just the paid months.

performance_obligations.name: Implementation services (waived)
performance_obligations.description: Standard $40K implementation services bundled at no charge.
performance_obligations.rationale: Implementation is typically not separately identifiable from subscription onboarding for this offering; bundled value should be allocated to the combined performance obligation and recognized as the subscription is delivered.

performance_obligations.name: Expansion pricing lock (2026 rates through 2027)
performance_obligations.description: Customer option to purchase additional seats at 2026 prices through end of 2027.
performance_obligations.rationale: Constitutes a material right under ASC 606-10-55-41 if the locked price is below expected standalone selling price at exercise; a portion of transaction price must be allocated to this option and recognized when exercised or expired.

variable_consideration_flags.explanation: The 90-day anytime out clause may shorten the enforceable contract term under ASC 606-10-25-3 to effectively a rolling 90-day contract, which would dramatically alter revenue recognition and TCV recognized.

The locked 2026 seat-expansion pricing through 2027 is a material right requiring allocation of transaction price to the option based on estimated likelihood and discount vs. SSP at exercise (ASC 606-10-55-42).
6 months of free service prior to paid term is non-cash consideration tied to the contract; total $480K transaction price should be allocated across the entire service period including free months.
Waived $40K implementation has implied SSP and is bundled; transaction price allocation should reflect the combined deliverable.

contract_modification_risk.explanation: The 90-day termination-for-convenience clause materially limits enforceable rights and may require treating the contract as a series of shorter renewable periods. The expansion pricing lock could trigger a contract modification under ASC 606-10-25-10 when seats are added at locked-in prices below then-prevailing SSP.

recognized_revenue_schedule.notes: Allocated portion of $480K TCV ratably recognized during free access period (assuming enforceable 24-month term despite TFC).
Continued ratable recognition of allocated transaction price.
Ratable recognition over months 13-24; assumes TFC clause does not shorten enforceable term.

red_flags.label: 90-day termination-for-convenience clause
red_flags.explanation: May reduce enforceable contract term under ASC 606-10-25-3 to 90 days, collapsing TCV recognition and impacting commission amortization (ASC 340-40). Requires CFO/auditor pre-clearance.

red_flags.label: Expansion pricing lock creates material right
red_flags.explanation: Locked 2026 pricing through 2027 likely constitutes a material right requiring transaction price allocation to the option.

red_flags.label: 6-month free period + waived implementation
red_flags.explanation: Significant non-cash and bundled consideration; total transaction price must be re-allocated across all promised goods/services, not just the paid months.

red_flags.label: Steep 35% discount + competitive displacement
red_flags.explanation: Discount magnitude and bundled freebies may distort SSP analysis used elsewhere in the portfolio.

asc606.reasoning_summary: This deal combines multiple interacting variable-consideration sources: a 6-month free period, waived $40K implementation, a 90-day TFC clause that may shorten the enforceable term, and a 2026 expansion pricing lock through 2027 that creates a material right. Each element individually requires judgment; together they make revenue recognition timing and transaction price allocation highly uncertain. The TFC clause is the most consequential issue and could collapse the recognition schedule to a rolling short-term contract. This is an automated estimate; final determination requires CFO/auditor review.

flagged_clauses.customer_proposed_language: Customer requests a 90-day termination-for-convenience right exercisable at any time during the 24-month term, with no cure period or cause requirement.
flagged_clauses.risk_explanation: A 90-day anytime TFC on a 24-month deal means Clay absorbs the full cost of a 6-month free ramp plus $40K waived implementation, then faces walk risk from month 7 onward with only 90 days' notice. The mechanism: if Tessera churns at month 10, Clay collects ~$80K against a $40K implementation waiver and 6 months of free access — net revenue deeply negative versus cost to serve. Combined with a 35% discount, this clause converts a 24-month commitment into a de facto month-to-month arrangement after month 7.
flagged_clauses.suggested_counter: Section [X] – Termination for Convenience is deleted in its entirety. In lieu thereof: 'Customer may terminate this Agreement for convenience upon one hundred eighty (180) days' prior written notice, provided that (a) such notice may not be delivered prior to the end of month twelve (12) of the Initial Term, (b) all fees through the end of the notice period remain due and payable, and (c) no refund of prepaid fees shall be owed.' For the avoidance of doubt, termination rights during the Initial Term remain limited to termination for cause as defined in Section [Y].
flagged_clauses.fallback_position: Accept 90-day TFC but only exercisable after month 18 of the 24-month term, with all remaining annual fees for the notice-period year remaining due. Minimum cash collected before TFC window opens must cover implementation waiver and free-period cost basis.
flagged_clauses.precedent_notes: Clay has accepted post-month-12 TFC windows on select enterprise deals above $500K ACV; sub-$300K ACV deals with concurrent free periods and implementation waivers have not historically received TFC rights.

flagged_clauses.customer_proposed_language: Customer requests that all seat expansions purchased through end of 2027 be priced at 2026 rates, regardless of any price increases Clay implements in the interim.
flagged_clauses.risk_explanation: Tessera is explicitly on an upmarket growth trajectory (1,200 employees, second profitable quarter, new CRO with stated expansion mandate). A 2026 price lock through end of 2027 means any seat adds — potentially significant given their stated consolidation and hiring trajectory — are immunized from Clay's standard annual price escalation. If Clay raises list prices 10–15% in 2027 (typical SaaS), Clay foregoes that uplift on every expansion unit Tessera buys. The lock is also asymmetric: it applies to expansions but not to any downward renegotiation leverage Clay might otherwise have at renewal.
flagged_clauses.suggested_counter: Section [X] – Expansion Pricing: 'For any incremental seats added during the Initial Term, Customer shall receive pricing no greater than the per-seat rate in effect at the time of the original Order Form, subject to a maximum annual increase of five percent (5%) per calendar year. This expansion pricing commitment (a) applies solely to the same product tier purchased hereunder, (b) expires upon the end of the Initial Term, and (c) is contingent on Customer remaining current on all payment obligations. No expansion pricing commitment shall apply to any Renewal Term.'
flagged_clauses.fallback_position: Accept a flat 2026 price lock on expansions up to 20% incremental seat growth over the contract baseline; any expansion beyond 20% of baseline seats is priced at then-current list with a 10% loyalty discount.
flagged_clauses.precedent_notes: Clay has offered price-hold-for-term on expansions for strategic logos above $500K ACV; a hard calendar-year lock (extending into a renewal period) irrespective of ACV has not been standard.

flagged_clauses.customer_proposed_language: Months 1–6 of the 24-month agreement are entirely free (no payment obligation); the paid term begins at month 7, yielding a ramp schedule that collects $120K in year one against a $240K ACV.
flagged_clauses.risk_explanation: The 6-month free period, stacked on top of a 35% discount and a $40K implementation waiver, means Clay's effective yield in year one is $120K against a list value of $180K ($360K ACV ÷ 2). Combined with the 90-day TFC, Clay could exit the deal having collected $80K while having delivered 10 months of full service plus implementation. The free period also resets the customer's psychological 'start' of value realization to month 7, weakening renewal leverage at month 24.
flagged_clauses.suggested_counter: Section [X] – Ramp Period: 'Months 1 through 3 of the Initial Term shall be provided at no charge as an onboarding period ('Ramp Period'). Commencing on the first day of month 4, Customer shall be invoiced at the full Annual Contract Value of $240,000, payable annually in advance. The Ramp Period shall not count toward any termination notice windows or minimum commitment periods.' (Reduces free exposure from 6 months to 3 months, recovering $60K in year-one cash.)
flagged_clauses.fallback_position: Accept 4 months free (not 6), with the paid term beginning month 5. Annual invoicing at $240K ACV commences month 5. TFC window, if granted at all, cannot open until month 18.
flagged_clauses.precedent_notes: Clay has approved up to 3-month ramp periods on competitive displacement deals; 6-month free periods have been approved only on deals exceeding $400K ACV with no concurrent TFC right.

flagged_clauses.customer_proposed_language: Implementation services (standard list price $40,000) are waived entirely as part of the competitive displacement package.
flagged_clauses.risk_explanation: Individually a defensible concession on a competitive displacement deal; however, stacked with the 6-month free period and 35% discount, the total concession package is $40K waived services + $120K foregone ACV (free period) + $120K discount off list = $280K in total value given against $480K TCV. The $40K waiver becomes a direct cash cost (not a discount) if Clay uses third-party implementation resources. The risk compounds if the TFC is exercised early: Clay will have delivered full implementation value with no recovery mechanism.
flagged_clauses.suggested_counter: Section [X] – Professional Services: 'Implementation services (standard value $40,000) shall be provided at no additional charge, subject to the following conditions: (a) Customer must complete the onboarding kickoff within thirty (30) days of the Effective Date; (b) implementation credits are non-transferable and expire if not consumed within the first ninety (90) days of the Term; and (c) in the event Customer exercises any early termination right, Customer shall reimburse Clay for the pro-rated value of implementation services delivered, calculated as ($40,000 × months remaining in Initial Term ÷ 24).'
flagged_clauses.fallback_position: Waive implementation services unconditionally but cap scope: up to 80 hours of onboarding support only; any additional professional services at standard rates. Remove clawback requirement.
flagged_clauses.precedent_notes: Implementation waivers on competitive displacements are common at this ACV tier; clawback provisions tied to early termination have been accepted by mid-market customers in at least two prior deals.

standard_clauses_affirmed: Data residency: not flagged as non-standard
redline.one_line_summary: We cannot ship this deal as-is: the 90-day anytime TFC must be pushed to post-month-18 at minimum, the 6-month free period should be cut to 3 months, and the 2027 expansion price lock needs a 5% annual escalator and a seat-volume cap — otherwise we're funding a trial with an exit ramp.
redline.reasoning_summary: This deal combines four compounding concessions — 35% discount, 6-month free period, $40K waived implementation, and a 90-day anytime TFC — that individually are precedented but collectively create a scenario where Clay's effective year-one yield (~$80–120K) may not cover cost of delivery. The 90-day TFC is the primary block: at a 24-month deal with a back-loaded ramp, it functionally caps Clay's downside protection at whatever is collected before month 7 plus 90 days. The expansion price lock is the secondary risk given Tessera's stated growth trajectory and new CRO mandate — any 2027 seat expansion is a material revenue event that should not be locked at 2026 rates without a cap and annual escalator. Tessera's strong consolidation intent and competitive bake-off context give Clay meaningful leverage to hold on TFC and free-period length; the customer needs a winner, and Clay's displacement value proposition is strongest in this evaluation.

required_approvers.rationale: Default rule applies to all deals; AE Manager is the first approval gate.
Deal TCV of $480K does not exceed $1M; rule does not fire.
Effective discount of 33.3% exceeds the 25% threshold and requires CFO sign-off.
ACV of $240K falls within the $100K–$500K band, triggering RevOps review for deal structure consistency.

Redline Agent flagged overall_redline_priority as 'block' due to high-risk clauses (90-day TFC, 6-month free period, expansion pricing lock, waived implementation); Legal must resolve before advancement.

required_approvers.rule_triggered: Pricing guardrail block and ASC 606 complexity
required_approvers.rationale: Pricing Agent flagged 'block_without_approval' on discount (33.3% vs 30% threshold) and 12-month ramp (exceeds 3-month mid-market ceiling); ASC 606 Agent flagged 'block_without_approval' on TFC clause potentially collapsing contract term and revenue recognition; Finance must review before deal proceeds.

blockers_to_address_first: Gross margin absolute floor violation: estimated margin of ~10% is well below the 25% absolute floor. Deal structure must be remodeled before re-quote (e.g., extend term to 36 months, reduce discount to 20%, reinstate partial implementation fees, or remove 90-day TFC and shorten free period to 3 months).

90-day anytime termination-for-convenience clause must be modified before Legal sign-off: push TFC window to post-month-18 at earliest, or delete entirely and counter with a post-month-12 180-day notice window with fees-due-through-notice-period.

6-month free period must be shortened: reduce to 3 months maximum to comply with mid-market ramp guardrail (3-month ceiling) and recover ~$60K in year-one cash; current structure violates ASC 606 recognition simplicity and compounds TFC risk.

Expansion pricing lock through 2027 requires constraints: add a 5% annual escalator starting 2027, cap lock to 20% incremental seat growth, and expire lock at renewal; current open-ended lock creates unquantified material right allocation and renewal leverage loss.

ASC 606 revenue recognition uncertainty: 90-day TFC may collapse enforceable contract term to 90 days, fundamentally altering TCV and recognition schedule; CFO/auditor pre-clearance required before deal enters signature phase.

Pricing exception documentation: AE must submit formal exception request with competitive justification (Apollo + Outreach + ZoomInfo displacement, 10-day close window, three-vendor bake-off) to justify 33.3% discount exceeding mid-market 30% Finance threshold.
Implementation waiver recovery provisions: if implementation services are waived, include clawback language tied to early termination to recover pro-rated service value in event of TFC exercise.

Margin recovery path must be selected from Pricing Agent alternatives before deal re-quote: 36-month term at 20% discount, Year-1 ramp with escalator + usage floor, or co-marketing credit model with 3-month ramp and TFC deletion.
approval.one_line_summary: This deal cannot proceed as structured: gross margin of ~10% violates the 25% absolute floor, the 90-day anytime TFC + 6-month free period + expansion price lock are blocking redline issues, and the 33.3% discount + 12-month ramp require Finance sign-off. Route is AE Manager → Legal (blocker resolution mandatory) → RevOps + Finance + CFO (parallel), ~10 business days; AE must remodel deal economics (extend term, reduce discount, remove/relocate TFC, shorten ramp) and obtain margin pre-clearance before Legal will clear redlines.
approval.reasoning_summary: All three upstream agents (Pricing, ASC 606, Redline) surfaced material blockers that must be resolved before the deal enters the standard approval chain. The Pricing Agent's 'block_absolute' on 10% gross margin is a hard stop — no approval authority can waive the 25% floor, and the deal must be restructured. The Redline Agent's 'block' priority is anchored on the 90-day anytime TFC, which when combined with a 6-month free period and 35% discount, creates a scenario where Clay funds an extended trial with minimal downside protection. The ASC 606 Agent's 'block_without_approval' on the TFC clause's impact on contract term is a financial reporting risk that CFO/accounting must pre-clear. The approval chain reflects these sequencing needs: AE Manager opens, Legal owns the redline blockage and must clear it before deal advances, then RevOps + Finance + CFO review in parallel (Finance and CFO have veto authority given the pricing and margin risks). The 10-day expected cycle assumes Legal can negotiate TFC/ramp/lock to acceptable terms in 3–5 days; if customer will not move, deal stalls.

text.text: Deal Review: Apollo/Outreach/ZoomInfo Displacement — Tessera Health

fields.text:
*Approval Chain*
AE Manager → Legal (veto) → RevOps + Finance + CFO (parallel, ~10 biz days)

text.text:
*What the deal team needs to know:*

• *This deal is a hard stop as structured.* The ~10% gross margin violates the absolute 25% floor — no approval authority can waive this. The deal must be remodeled before re-quote. Pricing Agent alternatives on the table: (1) 36-month term at 20% discount + paid implementation, (2) Year-1 ramp with nominal ramp fee + Y2 escalator, or (3) co-marketing credit model with 3-month ramp and TFC deleted. Priya must select a path and resubmit.

• *The 90-day anytime termination-for-convenience clause is a redline block and an ASC 606 risk.* Combined with 6 months free + $40K waived implementation, Clay could collect ~$80K while absorbing full delivery cost if Tessera exits at month 10. Separately, the TFC may collapse the enforceable contract term to 90 days under ASC 606-10-25-3, fundamentally altering revenue recognition — CFO/auditor pre-clearance required. Counter: push TFC to post-month-18 at earliest, or delete and substitute a post-month-12 180-day notice window with fees due through notice period.

• *The 2026 expansion pricing lock through end of 2027 is uncapped and asymmetric.* Tessera has a new CRO with an explicit consolidation and growth mandate, two profitable quarters, and 1,200 employees — seat expansion is a near-certainty. Locking all expansion at 2026 rates with no volume cap immunizes material future revenue from standard escalation. Required constraints before Legal will clear: 5% annual escalator starting 2027, cap at 20% incremental seat growth, lock expires at renewal.

elements.text: AE: *Priya Shankar* (Mgr: Marcus Reilly)   |   Close target: May 14, 2026   |   review filed by Kiln · agent-driven

slack_post.plaintext_fallback:
Deal Review: Apollo/Outreach/ZoomInfo Displacement — Tessera Health (Mid-Market)
ACV $240K | TCV $480K | 33.3% discount | ~10% gross margin — HARD STOP, violates 25% absolute floor.
Three blockers: (1) deal economics must be remodeled before re-quote; (2) 90-day anytime TFC is a redline block and ASC 606 risk requiring CFO pre-clearance; (3) 2026 expansion price lock through 2027 needs a 5% escalator, 20% seat cap, and renewal expiry.
Approval chain: AE Manager → Legal (mandatory redline resolution) → RevOps + Finance + CFO in parallel (~10 business days).
AE: Priya Shankar | Mgr: Marcus Reilly | review filed by Kiln · agent-driven

ae_email_draft.subject: [Tessera Health] Deal-desk review complete — 3 blockers to fix before this can move
ae_email_draft.body_markdown:
Priya, pricing review is done and this deal is blocked in its current form — it cannot go to Legal or Finance without structural changes first.

The core problem: at ~10% gross margin with a 35% discount, 6-month free period, $40K waived implementation, and a 90-day anytime out clause, the deal violates the absolute 25% margin floor. No approval authority can waive that; the economics must change before we re-quote.

**Your next 3 actions (in order):**

1. **Remodel the deal structure.** Pick one of the three pricing alternatives and resubmit: (a) 36-month term at 20% discount + reinstate implementation fees, (b) nominal ramp fee of $10K/mo months 1–6 + Year 2 escalator at 25% discount, or (c) 3-month free period + co-marketing commitment at 25% discount — all three get margin above the floor. Option (a) is the cleanest path.
2. **Kill or relocate the 90-day TFC.** Legal will not clear redlines with an anytime out clause on a deal this discounted. Counter-propose: TFC exercisable only after month 18, with 180 days' notice and all remaining fees due. This is also the single biggest ASC 606 risk — the current clause may collapse the enforceable contract term to 90 days and blow up revenue recognition.
3. **File the formal discount exception request.** The 33.3% discount exceeds the 30% Finance approval threshold — you need a written competitive justification (Apollo + Outreach + ZoomInfo displacement, three-vendor bake-off, 10-day window) submitted before Finance will even open the file.

**Approval chain you're walking into:** AE Manager (Marcus) → Legal (blocker veto, must clear redlines) → RevOps + Finance + CFO in parallel (Finance and CFO both have veto authority). Expected cycle: ~10 business days — which means you have essentially zero margin given the May 14 close date. Get Marcus aligned on the restructured economics today so Legal can start the clock.

customer_email_draft.subject: Tessera Health × Clay — revised deal structure for your review
customer_email_draft.body_markdown:
Thank you for the thorough evaluation your team has run — consolidating Apollo, Outreach, and ZoomInfo into a single platform is exactly the kind of initiative Clay is built to support, and we want to make this work on a timeline that respects your decision window.

On pricing and term: we can hold the $240K ACV with annual upfront payment, but we need to restructure the ramp — our internal guardrails require we shorten the free onboarding period from 6 months to 3 months, meaning paid invoicing would begin at month 4 rather than month 7; this recovers roughly $60K in year-one cash and is a firm requirement for Finance sign-off at this discount depth.

On implementation services: we are prepared to waive the standard $40K implementation fee, subject to a clawback provision that applies only if a termination right is exercised early — if the engagement runs its full term, there is no clawback and the waiver stands unconditionally.

On the 90-day termination-for-convenience clause: we cannot accept an anytime TFC on a 24-month deal structured with a free period and implementation waiver — the combination creates a scenario our Finance and Legal teams will not clear. Our counter is a 180-day notice window exercisable no earlier than the end of month 12, with all fees through the notice period remaining due; this gives Tessera a meaningful exit path in year two while preserving the contractual commitment that makes the discount defensible.

On expansion pricing: we can offer a per-seat rate lock at 2026 prices for expansions up to 20% of your baseline seat count, subject to a 5% annual escalator starting January 2027, expiring at the end of the initial term — this protects your budget for organic growth while keeping the commitment bounded.

To keep your May 14 decision date viable, we need alignment on these four points by end of week so our Legal and Finance approvals can run in parallel — can we schedule 30 minutes Thursday to walk through the redlined order form together?

approval_review_one_pager.title: Apollo/Outreach/ZoomInfo Displacement — Tessera Health Deal Approval One-Pager

sections.heading: Headline
sections.content_markdown: Tessera Health (Series C healthtech, 1,200 employees, ~$95M ARR) is consolidating a $320K/yr Apollo + Outreach + ZoomInfo stack onto Clay in a three-vendor bake-off with a 10-day decision window. AE (Priya Shankar) has proposed $240K ACV / $480K TCV over 24 months with a 35% discount, 6-month free period, and waived $40K implementation — **deal cannot proceed as currently structured and must be remodeled before re-quote.**

sections.heading: Pricing Summary
sections.content_markdown:
| Metric | Value |
|---|---|
| List price | $360K ACV / $720K TCV |
| Proposed price | $240K ACV / $480K TCV |
| Effective discount | 33.3% |
| Estimated gross margin | ~10% |
| Absolute margin floor | 25% (**VIOLATED — hard stop**) |
| Additional concessions | $40K waived implementation + 6-month free period ($120K foregone) |
| Total value given | ~$280K against $480K TCV |

**Year-1 effective yield: ~$120K** (months 7–12 only); if TFC exercised at month 10, Clay collects ~$80K net against full implementation and ramp costs.

**Best alternative structure (Pricing Agent rec):** 36-month term, 20% discount ($288K ACV), reinstate $40K implementation, 3-month free period, remove TFC → margin recovers to ~25% (floor-compliant).

sections.heading: Risk Findings
sections.content_markdown:
**🔴 BLOCK — Gross Margin Absolute Floor (Pricing Agent)**
- Estimated margin ~10% vs. 25% absolute floor. Hard stop; no approval authority can waive. Must restructure before re-quote.

**🔴 BLOCK — 90-Day Anytime Termination-for-Convenience (Redline + ASC 606)**
- TFC exercisable at any point in a 24-month deal means Clay's downside is capped at whatever is collected before month 7 + 90 days. Could net negative versus cost to serve.
- ASC 606: May collapse enforceable contract term to 90 days (ASC 606-10-25-3), fundamentally altering TCV recognition and commission amortization (ASC 340-40). **Requires CFO/auditor pre-clearance.**
- Counter: Delete TFC; offer post-month-12 termination with 180-day notice and fees due through notice period.

**🔴 BLOCK — 33.3% Discount + 12-Month Ramp (Pricing Agent)**
- Discount exceeds 30% mid-market Finance sign-off threshold and 20% soft ceiling.
- 12-month ramp exceeds 3-month mid-market ceiling — Finance approval required; also raises ASC 606 recognition complexity.

**🟡 WARN — Expansion Pricing Lock Through 2027 (Redline + ASC 606)**
- Tessera has a new CRO with explicit expansion mandate; locking 2026 seat pricing through end of 2027 forfeits standard 10–15% annual escalation on all future seat adds.
- ASC 606: Constitutes a material right (ASC 606-10-55-41) requiring transaction price allocation to the option.
- Counter: Cap lock to 20% incremental seat growth; add 5% annual escalator; expire at Initial Term end.

**🟡 WARN — 6-Month Free Period (Redline + ASC 606)**
- Stacked with TFC, Clay could deliver 10 months of service + full implementation and collect ~$80K. Precedent: free periods >3 months approved only on deals >$400K ACV with no concurrent TFC.
- ASC 606: Total $480K TCV must be allocated ratably across full service period (including free months), not just paid months.
- Counter: Reduce free period to 3 months; recover ~$60K in year-one cash.

**🟡 WARN — Waived $40K Implementation (Redline)**
- Direct cash cost if third-party resources used; no recovery mechanism if TFC exercised early.
- Counter: Add clawback language — pro-rated implementation value recoverable upon early termination.

sections.heading: Approval Routing
sections.content_markdown:
| Step | Approver | Veto? | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | AE Manager — Marcus Reilly | No | Default gate; must open immediately given 10-day close window |
| 2 | **Legal** | **Yes** | Must resolve TFC, free period, expansion lock redlines before deal advances — estimated 3–5 days |
| 3 (parallel) | RevOps | No | Deal structure consistency; ACV $240K triggers $100K–$500K band review |
| 3 (parallel) | **Finance** | No | Discount >30% threshold + 12-month ramp block; formal exception request with competitive justification required |
| 3 (parallel) | **CFO** | **Yes** | Discount >25% + ASC 606 TFC contract-term risk; auditor pre-clearance on revenue recognition required |

**Estimated cycle time: ~10 business days** — contingent on Legal clearing redlines within 3–5 days. If customer will not move on TFC or free period length, deal stalls. AE must remodel deal economics and submit pricing exception documentation before Legal review begins.

sections.heading: Recommendation
sections.content_markdown:
**🔴 HOLD — restructure required before re-quote.**

Deal is commercially attractive (genuine consolidation intent, competitive bake-off urgency, strong Tessera growth signals) but non-executable as structured: the 90-day anytime TFC combined with a 6-month free period, 35% discount, and ~10% gross margin violates the absolute margin floor and creates an uncapped trial-funding risk that no approval authority can waive — AE must remodel to the 36-month / 20% discount / 3-month ramp / TFC-deleted structure before this enters the approval chain.

comms.reasoning_summary: Calibrated customer email to firm tone given the deal context. AE email leads with verdict (ship-with-conditions (blockers must clear first)) and a numbered next-step list. One-pager and Slack post both headline the recommendation and surface 4 flagged redlines (4 countered in customer reply). Approval routing summary: ~10 business days through 5-step chain.

synthesis: Pricing is non-executable as structured: a 33.3% discount, 12-month ramp, and ~10% estimated gross margin breach the absolute 25% margin floor that no approver can waive. ASC 606 flags the 90-day TFC as the most consequential issue, with four compounding variable-consideration elements that could collapse recognition into a rolling short-term contract and trigger contract-modification risk. Redline rates the deal a block, anchored on that same 90-day anytime TFC stacked with a 6-month free period and waived $40K implementation, leaving year-one yield (~$80–120K) potentially below cost of delivery. Approval routes ae_manager → legal → rev_ops → finance → cfo over ~10 business days, but Legal must clear the TFC and margin restructure before the chain can meaningfully advance. Recommendation: hold and re-scope — reanchor discount to the 11–16% precedent band, cap or remove the TFC, and add an escalator to the expansion price lock before re-entering approval.

signals.headline: Tessera Health names new Chief Revenue Officer to lead consolidation push
signals.summary: Tessera Health announced the appointment of a new CRO (formerly VP Sales at a rival healthtech provider), tasked with consolidating sales tooling and accelerating mid-market enterprise expansion through 2026.

signals.headline: Tessera Health expands HIPAA-attested integration suite for hospital networks
signals.summary: Tessera released expanded HIPAA-attested integrations targeting Epic, Cerner, and Meditech-shaped buyers. The release is positioned as evidence of compliance maturity ahead of Tessera's enterprise upmarket push.

signals.headline: Tessera Health crosses 1,200 employees, posts second consecutive profitable quarter
signals.summary: Q4 2025 earnings update notes a 1,200-person headcount and a second straight profitable quarter, alongside a stated 2026 priority of vendor consolidation across go-to-market infrastructure.

signals.headline: Tessera Health adds VP RevOps from data-platform vendor
signals.summary: Tessera hired a VP RevOps from a data-platform vendor; her stated 90-day plan reportedly includes a sales-tooling RFP focused on prospecting, enrichment, and outbound orchestration.

signals.headline: Healthtech RevOps leaders cite stack consolidation as #1 2026 priority — survey
signals.summary: Industry survey finds 64% of mid-market healthtech RevOps leaders plan to consolidate three or more sales-engagement tools in 2026, citing renewal pressure and security-review fatigue.
